item.title:
US–CHINA TRADE TALKS IN PARIS SHOW PROGRESS
U.S. and Chinese officials held “candid and constructive” talks in Paris, agreeing to work toward greater stability in their trade relationship, according to sources familiar with the discussions.
Both sides discussed possible increases in Chinese purchases of U.S. agricultural goods ahead of the upcoming Trump–Xi summit in Beijing. U.S. officials also raised the need for China to buy more Boeing aircraft, coal, oil, and gas.
The talks explored managed trade and investment mechanisms for leaders to consider at the summit, and addressed U.S. firms’ difficulties obtaining rare earths such as yttrium.
Technical-level negotiations will continue Monday to refine the details.
item.description:
US–CHINA TRADE TALKS IN PARIS SHOW PROGRESS

U.S. and Chinese officials held “candid and constructive” talks in Paris, agreeing to work toward greater stability in their trade relationship, according to sources familiar with the discussions.

Both sides discussed possible increases in Chinese purchases of U.S. agricultural goods ahead of the upcoming Trump–Xi summit in Beijing. U.S. officials also raised the need for China to buy more Boeing aircraft, coal, oil, and gas.

The talks explored managed trade and investment mechanisms for leaders to consider at the summit, and addressed U.S. firms’ difficulties obtaining rare earths such as yttrium.

Technical-level negotiations will continue Monday to refine the details.

item.title:
BAHRAIN SMELTER CUTS OUTPUT AS HORMUZ DISRUPTS SUPPLIES
Aluminium Bahrain BSC, operator of the world’s largest single-site aluminum smelter, has begun a phased shutdown of three production lines — about 19% of its 1.6 million-ton annual capacity — to conserve raw materials as shipping through the Strait of Hormuz slows sharply.
The move highlights growing disruption across the Middle East aluminum industry. Supply bottlenecks have pushed prices on the London Metal Exchange up more than 9% since the Iran conflict began, reaching their highest level since 2022.
Regional smelters are struggling to ship metal and secure alumina feedstock, while Alba has already suspended sales to customers amid mounting logistics and energy constraints.
item.description:
BAHRAIN SMELTER CUTS OUTPUT AS HORMUZ DISRUPTS SUPPLIES

Aluminium Bahrain BSC, operator of the world’s largest single-site aluminum smelter, has begun a phased shutdown of three production lines — about 19% of its 1.6 million-ton annual capacity — to conserve raw materials as shipping through the Strait of Hormuz slows sharply.

The move highlights growing disruption across the Middle East aluminum industry. Supply bottlenecks have pushed prices on the London Metal Exchange up more than 9% since the Iran conflict began, reaching their highest level since 2022.

Regional smelters are struggling to ship metal and secure alumina feedstock, while Alba has already suspended sales to customers amid mounting logistics and energy constraints.

item.title:
OIL MARKETS BRACE FOR VOLATILITY AFTER KHARG STRIKE
Oil markets are set for a turbulent week after a US strike on Iran’s key export hub, Kharg Island, heightened fears of supply disruption across the Middle East.
President Donald Trump warned attacks could expand to energy infrastructure if Iran blocks the Strait of Hormuz, while Tehran threatened retaliation against US-linked facilities.
Brent crude surged 11% last week, briefly hitting $119.50 a barrel, and analysts expect further volatility as the fate of Kharg — vital to Iran’s exports — remains unclear.
Shipping through Hormuz is near standstill, pressuring global energy flows and pushing fuel prices higher worldwide.
item.description:
OIL MARKETS BRACE FOR VOLATILITY AFTER KHARG STRIKE

Oil markets are set for a turbulent week after a US strike on Iran’s key export hub, Kharg Island, heightened fears of supply disruption across the Middle East.

President Donald Trump warned attacks could expand to energy infrastructure if Iran blocks the Strait of Hormuz, while Tehran threatened retaliation against US-linked facilities.

Brent crude surged 11% last week, briefly hitting $119.50 a barrel, and analysts expect further volatility as the fate of Kharg — vital to Iran’s exports — remains unclear.

Shipping through Hormuz is near standstill, pressuring global energy flows and pushing fuel prices higher worldwide.

item.title: *ALUMINIUM BAHRAIN SUSPENDS 19% OF TOTAL PRODUCTION CAPACITY
item.description: *ALUMINIUM BAHRAIN SUSPENDS 19% OF TOTAL PRODUCTION CAPACITY

item.title: R to @DeItaone: Source: https://www.nbcnews.com/politics/donald-trump/iran-negotiate-ceasefire-deal-trump-kharg-hormuz-oil-rcna263474
item.description: Source: nbcnews.com/politics/donald-…

item.title:
HORMUZ OIL FLOWS NEAR STANDSTILL, GOLDMAN SACHS SAYS
Goldman Sachs says oil shipments through the Strait of Hormuz have nearly stopped.
Based on vessel tracking, flows have fallen from about 19.5 million barrels per day to just 0.5 million barrels per day over the past few days. After accounting for pipelines redirecting some supply, the net disruption to Persian Gulf exports is estimated at about 17.2 million barrels per day.
Tracking is difficult, but two independent vessel-count sources showed no oil tankers crossing the strait on March 12. Reuters reported that Iran did allow two India-flagged LPG carriers to pass, with one escorted by the Indian Navy.
Goldman says flows could recover through three scenarios: Iran selectively allowing some tankers through (such as those linked to China or India), a broader de-escalation of the conflict, or stronger naval protection for shipping.
However, the bank warns recovery could take longer than expected. So far, none of the reported tanker attacks have involved Asian-flagged ships, which is significant since Asian buyers account for the majority of Hormuz oil shipments.
item.description:
HORMUZ OIL FLOWS NEAR STANDSTILL, GOLDMAN SACHS SAYS

Goldman Sachs says oil shipments through the Strait of Hormuz have nearly stopped.

Based on vessel tracking, flows have fallen from about 19.5 million barrels per day to just 0.5 million barrels per day over the past few days. After accounting for pipelines redirecting some supply, the net disruption to Persian Gulf exports is estimated at about 17.2 million barrels per day.

Tracking is difficult, but two independent vessel-count sources showed no oil tankers crossing the strait on March 12. Reuters reported that Iran did allow two India-flagged LPG carriers to pass, with one escorted by the Indian Navy.

Goldman says flows could recover through three scenarios: Iran selectively allowing some tankers through (such as those linked to China or India), a broader de-escalation of the conflict, or stronger naval protection for shipping.

However, the bank warns recovery could take longer than expected. So far, none of the reported tanker attacks have involved Asian-flagged ships, which is significant since Asian buyers account for the majority of Hormuz oil shipments.

item.title:
TRUMP: IRAN READY FOR CEASEFIRE — BUT “TERMS NOT GOOD ENOUGH”
President Donald Trump said Iran is willing to negotiate a ceasefire but he refuses to make a deal yet, saying the terms are “not good enough.”
In a phone interview with NBC News, Trump said any agreement would likely require Iran to completely abandon nuclear ambitions, though he declined to reveal specific conditions.
He also said the U.S. is working with other countries to secure the Strait of Hormuz as oil prices rise, and dismissed concerns about higher gas prices in the U.S.
Trump claimed recent U.S. strikes have destroyed much of Iran’s missile and drone capabilities and said Kharg Island was “totally demolished,” though energy infrastructure was spared.
He also questioned whether Iran’s new supreme leader, Mojtaba Khamenei, is even alive, calling reports of his death a rumor.
Separately, Trump criticized Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelenskyy, saying he is harder to negotiate with than Russian President Vladimir Putin as the U.S. tries to end the war in Ukraine.
item.description:
TRUMP: IRAN READY FOR CEASEFIRE — BUT “TERMS NOT GOOD ENOUGH”

President Donald Trump said Iran is willing to negotiate a ceasefire but he refuses to make a deal yet, saying the terms are “not good enough.”

In a phone interview with NBC News, Trump said any agreement would likely require Iran to completely abandon nuclear ambitions, though he declined to reveal specific conditions.

He also said the U.S. is working with other countries to secure the Strait of Hormuz as oil prices rise, and dismissed concerns about higher gas prices in the U.S.

Trump claimed recent U.S. strikes have destroyed much of Iran’s missile and drone capabilities and said Kharg Island was “totally demolished,” though energy infrastructure was spared.

He also questioned whether Iran’s new supreme leader, Mojtaba Khamenei, is even alive, calling reports of his death a rumor.

Separately, Trump criticized Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelenskyy, saying he is harder to negotiate with than Russian President Vladimir Putin as the U.S. tries to end the war in Ukraine.
